content: JavaScript is a

language — variables do not have a fixed type, values do. The same variable can hold a string, then a number, then an object. TypeScript adds static type annotations on top of JavaScript's runtime type system, but understanding what happens at runtime remains essential for Angular development.
Every JavaScript value is either a
or an
. This distinction drives how values are compared, copied, and how Angular's change detection decides to re-render.

attrs.alt: JavaScript type system: primitives, objects, typeof, value vs reference

content: There are seven primitive types. Primitives are
— you cannot change a string character in place, only replace the binding with a new string. They are stored by
— assigning a primitive to a new variable copies the value.

content.text: Strings represent Unicode text. JavaScript uses UTF-16 internally. String literals use single quotes, double quotes, or backticks (template literals).

content: JavaScript has a single numeric type for both integers and floating-point values: IEEE 754 64-bit double precision. This is why
— a floating-point rounding artefact.
BigInt (ES2020) handles integers beyond
. Use it for database IDs from 64-bit systems, cryptography, and financial calculations requiring exact integers.

content.text:
const bigId = 9007199254740993n;       // BigInt literal — 'n' suffix
const big   = BigInt('9007199254740993'); // from string

bigId + 1n        // 9007199254740994n
// bigId + 1      // TypeError — cannot mix BigInt and number
Number(bigId)     // loses precision for values > MAX_SAFE_INTEGER

content: Exactly two values:
and
. Every JavaScript expression has a truthy/falsy interpretation when used in a boolean context.

content.text:
// Falsy values — evaluate to false in boolean context
false, 0, -0, 0n, '', "", ``, null, undefined, NaN

// Truthy — everything else, including:
'0'    // non-empty string
[]     // empty array
{}     // empty object

// Double negation coerces to boolean
!!0       // false
!!''      // false
!![]      // true  ← a common gotcha
!!'0'     // true  ← '0' is truthy even though Number('0') is falsy

// Boolean() is clearer for explicit conversion
Boolean(0)   // false
Boolean([])  // true

content: Angular templates evaluate expressions in a boolean context for
. An empty array is truthy — checking

content.text: does NOT hide the element when the list is empty. Use

content: means a value has not been assigned.
means an intentional absence of value. Both are falsy. The practical distinction:

content.text:
let x;           // x is undefined
let y = null;    // y is explicitly null

x == null   // true  — loose equality: undefined == null
x === null  // false — strict equality
y === null  // true

// Nullish coalescing — checks for null OR undefined (not 0 or '')
const name = user.name ?? 'Guest';   // 'Guest' only if null/undefined
const count = user.count ?? 0;       // 0 only if null/undefined, NOT when 0

// Optional chaining — short-circuits at null/undefined
const city = user?.address?.city;   // undefined, not TypeError

Symbols are guaranteed-unique values. Their primary use is as object property keys that cannot accidentally collide with other property names — useful in meta-programming and creating well-known protocol hooks.
Everything that is not a primitive is an object. Objects are mutable and stored by reference — two variables can point to the same underlying object.

content: Arrays are objects with integer keys and a
property. They are the most-used data structure in Angular templates.
Unlike plain objects,
accepts any type as a key (including objects) and remembers insertion order.
stores unique values.

content.text: JavaScript automatically converts types in many expressions. Understanding coercion prevents subtle bugs.

// String concatenation vs addition — + is overloaded
'5' + 3    // '53' — number coerced to string
'5' - 3    // 2   — string coerced to number (- is only arithmetic)
'5' * '3'  // 15  — both coerced to number
true + 1   // 2   — true coerces to 1
false + 1  // 1   — false coerces to 0
null + 1   // 1   — null coerces to 0
undefined + 1 // NaN — undefined coerces to NaN

// Comparison coercion
'5' == 5   // true  — loose equality coerces
'5' === 5  // false — strict equality, no coercion

// To string
String(42)         // '42'
(42).toString()    // '42'
`${42}`            // '42'

// To number
Number('42')       // 42
Number('3.14')     // 3.14
Number('')         // 0     ← surprising
Number(null)       // 0     ← surprising
Number(undefined)  // NaN
Number(true)       // 1
parseInt('42px')   // 42    — parses leading digits
parseFloat('3.14abc') // 3.14

// To boolean
Boolean(0)         // false
Boolean('')        // false
Boolean(null)      // false
Boolean([])        // true  ← common gotcha

content: Always use
(strict equality) in Angular TypeScript code. The Angular ESLint config enables
by default, which errors on
outside of
null-checks.

content.text:
// typeof — works for primitives (with the null bug)
typeof 'hello'     // 'string'
typeof 42          // 'number'
typeof true        // 'boolean'
typeof undefined   // 'undefined'
typeof Symbol()    // 'symbol'
typeof 42n         // 'bigint'
typeof null        // 'object'  ← historical bug, null !== object
typeof {}          // 'object'
typeof []          // 'object'  ← not 'array'
typeof function(){} // 'function'

// Better checks
Array.isArray([])     // true
value === null        // null check
value instanceof Map  // true for Map instances

// Reliable type check via Object.prototype.toString
Object.prototype.toString.call([])     // '[object Array]'
Object.prototype.toString.call(null)   // '[object Null]'
Object.prototype.toString.call(/x/)    // '[object RegExp]'

TypeScript adds compile-time type annotations. At runtime, types are erased — the JavaScript that runs has no type information. TypeScript types exist only to help tooling catch mistakes before execution.
Type guards narrow a union type in a conditional block:
Value vs Reference — Angular Change Detection Implications
Angular's default change detection compares object references, not deep content. This has practical consequences for how you update state.

content: Immutability is not just a style preference in Angular — it is required for
change detection and makes NgRx/Signal-based state management reliable. Always return new object/array references instead of mutating.

content.text: Loose equality coerces types silently. Use
is true for an empty array. Use
Angular won't detect the change. Return new references.

content: Object spread creates a
copy — nested objects are still shared by reference. When you need a fully independent deep copy:

content.text:
// String is iterable — for...of iterates code points, not UTF-16 units
const emoji = '👋🏽';
emoji.length;  // 4 — two surrogate pairs
[...emoji];    // ['👋', '🏽'] — two code points via spread/for...of

for (const char of 'café') console.log(char); // c, a, f, é

// String methods relevant to Angular templates
const url = '  /api/users/42  ';
url.trim()          // '/api/users/42'
url.trimStart()     // '/api/users/42  '
'hello'.padStart(8) // '   hello'
'hello'.padEnd(8, '-') // 'hello---'
'abc'.repeat(3)     // 'abcabcabc'
'a,b,c'.split(',')  // ['a', 'b', 'c']
['a','b','c'].join('-') // 'a-b-c'
'Hello World'.toLowerCase() // 'hello world'
'hello world'.at(-1)  // 'd' — negative indexing (ES2022)

Math.floor(4.9)    // 4
Math.ceil(4.1)     // 5
Math.round(4.5)    // 5
Math.trunc(4.9)    // 4  — removes fractional part, towards zero
Math.abs(-5)       // 5
Math.max(1, 5, 3)  // 5
Math.min(1, 5, 3)  // 1
Math.pow(2, 10)    // 1024
2 ** 10            // 1024  — exponentiation operator
Math.sqrt(16)      // 4
Math.log(Math.E)   // 1
Math.PI            // 3.14159265358979...
Math.random()      // [0, 1) — use crypto.getRandomValues() for security
Working with null and undefined in Angular
Strict Null Checks in Angular Components
Angular's template syntax is JavaScript-like but has some differences. Understanding the type of each binding helps write correct templates.
String coercion — any value is stringified
Must be iterable — array, Set, string

@if (items) {
    ...
} @if (items.length) {
    ...
} @if (user$ | async; as user) {

{{ user.name }}

}

content: JavaScript has seven primitive types (
) and one object type. Primitives are immutable and copied by value; objects are mutable and copied by reference. Type coercion is implicit in JavaScript — always use

to avoid surprises. TypeScript's type annotations catch type errors at compile time but are erased at runtime. Angular's OnPush change detection relies on reference inequality to detect changes — return new array and object references rather than mutating in place.
